target.title: #92664: [Bug]: read tool fails to read GBK-encoded text files on Chinese Windows (displays garbled text)
target.body:
## Summary

- Fix garbled text when reading non-UTF-8 files (GBK on Chinese Windows,
  Shift_JIS on Japanese Windows) by reusing the existing Windows active-
  codepage decoder (`decodeWindowsOutputBuffer`) in the read tool.
- No new API parameter — the read tool follows the same encoding policy
  as subprocess output decoding (strict UTF-8 first, Windows codepage
  fallback on win32, UTF-8 replacement on other platforms).
- Removes the hard-coded cross-platform CJK encoding fallback order that
  ClawSweeper identified as a P1 issue (GBK can silently return plausible-
  but-wrong text for Shift_JIS/Big5/EUC-KR files before their correct
  decoder is tried).

Fixes #92664

## Linked context

- Issue #92664: Read tool fails to read GBK-encoded text files on Chinese
  Windows (displays garbled text).
- ClawSweeper review on #92700 identified P1 correctness issue with
  cross-platform CJK encoding guessing order.
- Existing `decodeWindowsOutputBuffer` in `src/infra/windows-encoding.ts`
  already handles Windows codepage-aware decoding for subprocess output.
  This PR reuses that for the read tool.
- Alternative PR #90116 (original read tool implementation) is the
  baseline — this PR follows the narrower maintainer-recommended path.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: The read tool now decodes text files using the
existing Windows active-codepage fallback from `windows-encoding.ts`
instead of a hard-coded cross-platform CJK encoding order. On Windows
with GBK code page 936, non-UTF-8 files are decoded as GBK. On Windows
with Shift_JIS code page 932, they are decoded as Shift_JIS. On non-
Windows platforms, behavior is unchanged (UTF-8 with replacement
characters).

**Real setup tested**: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64, Node.js v22.21.1,
OpenClaw main @ b130251b5e, vitest for test suites.

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/sessions/tools/read.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.read.host-tilde-expansion.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.read.workspace-root-guard.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/windows-encoding.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
✓ src/agents/sessions/tools/read.test.ts (5 tests | 5 passed)
  - reads managed inbound media refs as image files
  - shell-quotes the long-first-line fallback path
  - clamps non-positive line limits before slicing file content
  - decodes UTF-8 text files correctly (no regression)           ← NEW
  - does not crash on non-UTF-8 files (produces output)          ← NEW

✓ src/agents/agent-tools.read.host-tilde-expansion.test.ts (8 tests | 8 passed)
✓ src/agents/agent-tools.read.workspace-root-guard.test.ts (16 tests | 16 passed)
✓ src/infra/windows-encoding.test.ts (6 tests | 6 passed)
  - decodes GBK output on Windows when UTF-8 is invalid          ← existing
  - prefers valid UTF-8 output on Windows even when legacy CP    ← existing
  - keeps multibyte Windows codepage characters intact           ← existing
  - replays buffered UTF-8 lead bytes when split GBK             ← existing
  - keeps split valid UTF-8 output on the UTF-8 path             ← existing
```

All 35 tests pass (29 existing + 2 new encoding regression tests + 4
existing windows-encoding tests that cover the shared decoder).

**Observed result after the fix**: The read tool reuses the existing
well-tested `decodeWindowsOutputBuffer` policy. On Windows with GBK
code page 936, the test at `windows-encoding.test.ts:17` confirms
`decodeWindowsOutputBuffer` correctly decodes GBK bytes to "测试～；".
On non-Windows, the read tool gracefully produces output without
throwing (UTF-8 replacement characters — same as current behavior).

**What was not tested**: Live Chinese Windows read-tool session with
GBK files (cannot test in CI — requires Windows VM with Chinese locale
and code page 936). The existing windows-encoding tests already cover
the GBK decode path with simulated platform/codepage parameters.

**Proof limitations or environment constraints**: This is a Linux CI
environment. Full Windows GBK end-to-end validation requires a Chinese
Windows machine. The shared `decodeWindowsOutputBuffer` function is
already covered by unit tests with simulated Windows platform and GBK
codepage, and it is the same code path used by subprocess output
decoding (already battle-tested on real Windows).

## Tests and validation

```bash
# Read tool tests (existing + new encoding regression):
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/sessions/tools/read.test.ts

# Read tool host integration tests:
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.read.host-tilde-expansion.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.read.workspace-root-guard.test.ts

# Windows encoding decoder tests (shared by read tool and subprocess):
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/windows-encoding.test.ts

# Lint:
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/sessions/tools/read.ts src/infra/windows-encoding.ts
```

New tests added to `read.test.ts`:
- "decodes UTF-8 text files correctly (no regression)" — verifies valid
  UTF-8 Chinese ("你好世界") passes through unchanged.
- "does not crash on non-UTF-8 files (produces output through fallback)"
  — verifies GBK bytes don't throw and ASCII prefix survives any fallback
  path.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)
- On Chinese Windows: GBK text files now display correctly instead of
  mojibake (behavior change in the `read` tool output).
- On Japanese Windows: Shift_JIS text files now display correctly.
- On non-Windows: no change (UTF-8 with replacement characters as before).

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)
- No new config, no migration, same read API contract.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)
- Pure text decoding change. No new dependencies, no network, no secrets.

What is the highest-risk area?
- Changing the encoding policy could affect how non-UTF-8 files are
  presented to models on Windows. The fallback now follows the system
  code page rather than a fixed CJK order. On misconfigured Windows
  systems where the active code page does not match the actual file
  encoding, the model may still see garbled text (same as current
  behavior — this PR does not make that worse).

How is that risk mitigated?
- Reuses the existing battle-tested `decodeWindowsOutputBuffer` function
  that has been in production for subprocess output decoding on Windows
  since PR #30652.
- Strict UTF-8 is always tried first (preserves current behavior for the
  vast majority of files).
- Windows-only: the fallback only activates on win32, consistent with
  the existing subprocess output decoding policy.
- Covered by 6 existing windows-encoding tests + 2 new read tool encoding
  regression tests.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- ClawSweeper re-review requested (triggered after body update + push).

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?
- Waiting for ClawSweeper re-review on the narrowed approach.
- Live Chinese Windows end-to-end proof is not possible in CI but the
  shared decoder path is already covered by unit tests.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
- ✅ [P1] Narrow fallback to the active Windows encoding — replaced
  hard-coded CJK order with `decodeWindowsOutputBuffer`.
- ✅ [P2] Move repro into focused tests — deleted `scripts/repro-92664.mjs`,
  added focused regression tests to `read.test.ts`.
- ✅ Duplicate `decodeStrictUtf8` removed — imported from
  `windows-encoding.ts` where it already exists.
- ✅ Data model change concern — the serialized state change is
  limited to the read tool's import graph (no new stored fields).
target.author: zhiqiang26
